title: Coding Exercise
text: Introduction

title: Coding Exercise
text:
Preface
This is the website for a coding exercise about an environmental issue in a programming course, provided as part of on-site interview. This exercise is a modified version of a couple of existing lessons provided in EA 078 - Environmental Data Visualization.






This book is created using the Quarto publishing system in order to provide an example of its use in environmental data visualization and as a course resource tool for display of coding examples and visualization. The underlying code is hosted on github



The scholarly research presentation is here.

title: Coding Teaching Demo Assignment
text: Learning Outcomes

title: Coding Teaching Demo Assignment
text:
Installation and Orientation to R and RStudio
Learn basic coding instructions
Generate maps using R code
Generate environmental visualizations
Learn about a function

title: 1  Coding - Basics
text:
1.1 Coding - most Basic
Coding is a set of precise text-based instructions to a computer. Each programming language has its own set of syntax, vocabulary, and jargon.
It is a foreign language.
Today, I’ll teach you in R, which is my current preferred language. Data visualization is a great place to start with R programming, because the payoff is relatively immediate. You get to make informative and potentially elegant visualizations to help you understand data.

title: 1  Coding - Basics
text:
1.2 Stepwise R Data Science Framework

Step 0 - Install R and RStudio
Step 1 - Open RStudio 

Step 2 - Packages
A. Install key packages
B. Load key packages

Step 3 - Acquire and/or Load Data
A. Identify the path to the data
B. Identify the data format
C. Choose the right function to load the data - go to Step 2A and 2B again as needed D. Write code to import the data
E. Run the code to import the data

Check for Error messages and warning messages in console; if failure, go back to Step 2D

Check to make sure data is loaded (look in Environment window)
F. Look at the data - did it import correctly?
Check column headers

Check data types
G. Repeat Step 2 as needed for any other data required for visualization


Step 3 - Tidy the data Advanced data science
Step 4 - Visualize the data
A. Choose the visualization type
B. Choose the right functions
C. Write code to do a basic visualization
D. Add code to improve the visualization (repeat as needed)
E. Annotate labels, axes, points, legends
F. Export or publish the visualization

Step 5 - Communicate with your audience using the visualization A. Get feedback from audience B. Revise visualization (Step 4D as needed) to improve for intended audience

title: 1  Coding - Basics
text:
1.3 RStudio IDE orientation






Note



Opening RStudio loads R.
Opening R will not load RStudio.


Figure 1.1 shows an annotated image of RStudio with the four panels labeled. In the default layout, the top-left is the text editor panel, the bottom-left is the console panel, the top-right is the files, plots, and packages panel, and the bottom-right is the environment panel.






Figure 1.1: annotated.RStudio




Text Editor Panel - This is where you can enter code and have the editor color code it.
Console Panel - This is where errors and warnings appear when you run code. It can also be used to do direct coding, which I don’t recommend for beginners.
Files, plots, and packages panels - This is where files loaded in the working directory and packages in the default R directory are organized.
Environment Panel - This is where data and variables you define in your coding will be organized

title: 2  Maps in R
text:
2.1 Install Packages
Packages are collections of functions that accomplish specialized tasks. To make a map today, we’ll need to install three packages that are not part of base R.
Packages need to be installed locally the first time they are used.
install.packages('dplyr')
install.packages('sf')
install.packages('leaflet')
Next, we load the packages to make sure they are available within the R environment.
library(dplyr)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)

title: 2  Maps in R
text:
dplyr - data manipulation
sf - spatial data manipulation
leaflet - interactive, mobile-friendly maps

title: 2  Maps in R
text:
2.3 Visualize the Data

2.3.1 Choose the visualization type and function
We’ll useleaflet() for visualization for geospatial data today, and add at least one add function to display spatial information.
Vocabulary

leaflet() - create a leaflet map

addTiles() - add map tiles

addMarkers() - add point markers

addPolygons() - add filled polygons

addLegends() - add a map legend



2.3.2 Write the code for a basic geospatial visualization

2.3.2.1 Example 1 - Basic Tile Map
Figure 2.2 shows the standard leaflet map with a minimal reproducible example.
The code is simply two lines with two actionable functions.

leaflet() |> #call a leaflet map
  addTiles() #with default map tiles







Figure 2.2: A very basic Leaflet map






2.3.2.2 Example 2. Add Environmental information

Make a leaflet map with the SoCalEJ dataset. It should look like Figure 2.3.


leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ)  #NEW







Figure 2.3: Leaflet map with Calenviroscreen 4.0







2.3.3 Improve the Visualization
In the addPolygons() function, there are many parameters that can be modified.

color
weight
opacity
fillColor
fillOpacity
stroke
group
label

Let’s iterate and make some improvements!
Figure 2.4 shows what happens we just change the color from blue to black.

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ, 
              color = 'black') #NEW 







Figure 2.4: Leaflet map with SoCalEJ census tracts color changed to black




The lines are too heavy. Let’s lower the weight - we’ll let the color go back to blue to keep the example minimal.
Figure 2.5 shows the result, which is a much cleaner look.

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ, 
              color = 'black',
              weight = 1)  #NEW







Figure 2.5: Leaflet map with line weight changed to 1.





2.3.3.1 Visualization Iteration
Within the SoCalEJ dataset are over 50 columns of values with names like “Ozone”, “Pesticide”, and “DieselPM_P”. We will use the fillcolor variable to indicate the values of one of these columns using a color palette.
Functions that help define the color palette in leaflet are:

colorNumeric() - a linear color scale
colorQuantile() - a color scale that can ensure the bin sizes are approximately equal
colorFactor() - a color scale that is good for categorical (‘non-numeric’) data


#NEW - color palette definition
pal1 <- colorNumeric(palette = 'magma', domain = SoCalEJ$DieselPM_P) 

Now we see what those two palettes look like in the map. Figure 2.6 shows the default numeric palette, but it is too faint with the background tiles.

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ,
              weight = 1,
              color = ~pal1(DieselPM_P) #NEW
              ) 







Figure 2.6: Leaflet map for Ozone in Southern California.





leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> #NEW 
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron)|> 
  addPolygons(color = ~pal1(DieselPM_P),
              weight = 1,
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = pal1, 
            title = 'Diesel Particulate Matter (%)', 
            values = ~DieselPM_P)







Figure 2.7: Leaflet map for with numeric palette for Diesel PM percentiles, color legend, and opacity optimized.




The SoCalEJ dataset has multiple columns that have values of -999 indicating missing data which will interfere with color scales.
Figure 2.8 is a map using the Lead_P variable instead of DieselPM_P. Lead is an indication of lead-based paint exposures based on housing age households with children under age 6.

palLead <- colorNumeric(palette = 'magma', domain = SoCalEJ$Lead_P, n = 5)

leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> 
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |> 
  setView(lat = 34, lng = -117.60, zoom = 9) |> 
  addPolygons(weight = 1,
              color = ~palLead(Lead_P),
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = palLead, 
            title = 'Lead (%)', 
            values = ~Lead_P)







Figure 2.8: Leaflet map for Lead Paint exposure percentile in Southern California




The -999 values make the visualization useless. We need to clean the dataset. A function can replace -999 values with NA.

title: 2  Maps in R
text:
2.4 Functions
A function can be created by defining a name, arguments, the code to execute the algorithm, and a return of the transformed dataset.

# General Structure
function_name <- function(data, argument1) {
  # Perform cleaning steps (e.g., replace -999 with NA)
  cleaned_data <- ... 
  return(cleaned_data)
}


2.4.1 Very Simple Example









2.4.1.1 Functional exercises

Try to design your own function called multiply_number
Try to design your own function that prints a user name that they enter.




2.4.2 Very complicated function
R has some non-standard issues when working with data frames. I’ll show this example but breaking it down is pretty advanced.
Start with a working example of replacing -999 values in the data frame.
The mutate() function creates a new variable. The if_else() function checks to see if a condition is TRUE or FALSE, then returns the second value if TRUE and the third value if FALSE.

SoCalEJ2 <- mutate(SoCalEJ, 
    # this searches for -999 values and replaces them with -999 for the Lead_P variable
            clean = if_else(Lead_P == -999, NA, Lead_P)
      )

This works!
However, to create the function that applies that code, we need to use non-standard evaluation embrace and some quotes to ensure proper evaluation within the code. This is an unsuitable example for beginners, but it does what I want to demonstrate in terms of quality controlling the table.

# name and arguments
replace999 <- function(df, var) {
  clean <- mutate(df, 
    "clean" = if_else({{ var }} == -999, NA, {{ var }}))
  # mutate() defines a new variable
  
  return(clean)
}



2.4.3 Apply the function

SoCalEJ <- replace999(SoCalEJ, Lead_P) 

This part is simple.


2.4.4 Rerun the map code
Figure 2.9 shows the update

##Note - the column name is now clean rather than Lead_P in the color palette and code
palLead <- colorNumeric(palette = 'magma', domain = SoCalEJ$clean, n = 5)

leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  setView(lat = 34, lng = -117.60, zoom = 9) |> 
  addPolygons(stroke = FALSE,
              fillColor = ~palLead(clean),
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = palLead, 
            title = 'Lead Paint (%)', 
            values = ~clean)







Figure 2.9: Clean Lead Paint exposure percentile in Southern California

title: Appendix A — R and RStudio Installation and Overview
text:
A.1 Download and Install R
R is a free software environment for statistical computing and graphics.
Current version 4.5.3 “Reassured Reassurer” was released March 11, 2026
RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It is available in open source and commercial editions. This lecture is using RStudio 2026.01.2+418.
Posit Cloud is an online version that has a free version but is mostly a subscription Softward-as-a-Service model of the same tools. This is an option for those who prefer licensing and throttled use features.
These tools will be the primary software used in this course to develop environmental visualizations. Alternate tools/software can be used for assignments and projects, but I will not devote class time to troubleshooting those tools/software.
R is maintained and made available through the web-page at The Comprehensive R Archive Network aka CRAN.
The top of the CRAN web page provides three links for downloading R. Follow the link that describes the OS of your computer: Mac, Windows, or Linux.
crumbs: A  R and RStudio Installation and Overview

title: Appendix A — R and RStudio Installation and Overview
text:
A.1.1 R for macOS
To install R on a Mac, click the “Download R for macOS” link. Next, click on the R-4.5.3 package link (or the package link for the most current release of R). An installer will download to guide you through the installation process. As a starting point, I recommend that most users should choose the default installation settings until they are familiar with R.


A.1.2 R for Windows
To install R on Windows, click the “Download R for Windows” link. Next, click on the install R for the first time link which contains base R. Then click on the link that says Download R-4.5.3 for Windows. A win.exe file will be downloaded to your downloads folder. Open that file after it downloads and follow the installation process. As a starting point, I recommend that most users should choose the default installation settings until they are familiar with R.


A.1.3 R for Linux
R comes preinstalled on many Linux systems, but update to the newest version of R if yours is out of date. The CRAN website provides files for different Linux builds including Debian, Fedora, Redhat, Suse, and Ubuntu. Choose the appropriate directory and the installation procedure for your flavor of Linux.
crumbs: A  R and RStudio Installation and Overview

title: Appendix A — R and RStudio Installation and Overview
text:
A.2 About R
R is not a friendly computer program like a web browser or a Microsoft Office product. R is a programming language, like C++, Python, or Java. R can be run like an old-school UNIX terminal, writing commands directly onto monitors with green fonts; imagine Neo in the Matrix or Angelina Jolie in Hackers. While one can still do that, most folks use an IDE.



Hackers use green fonts and dark mode.
crumbs: A  R and RStudio Installation and Overview

title: Appendix A — R and RStudio Installation and Overview
text:
A.3 RStudio IDE
RStudio is an application - like MS Word or a browser. However, Rstudio is an app that helps you write in the “R” language. It is literally a foreign language (unless you know it already). And just like any language, R has its own syntax, grammar, and vocabulary. It takes training and time to learn R, or any programming language
I recommend RStudio because it makes using R much easier. Also, the RStudio interface looks about the same for different operating systems, which will help me because I am a Windows user and I’ve observed that Claremont Colleges’ scholars tend to lean towards macOS.
Download RStudio desktop here - I recommend the free open source version. Make sure to pick the version that is appropriate for your OS. Then follow the installation instructions.
Once installed, RStudio can be opened like any other program or app on your computer; usually just click on the icon on the desktop. When you open it, it should look something like Figure C.1.






Figure A.1: RStudio



Here, there are four separate windows or “Panels”.

The source panel is top-left and this is basically a text editor where you type R code or regular text. Code here gets colored and looks different if it is doing various R things. More on this later. Code doesn’t get immediately executed here; it is more like a holding place for writing/testing/debugging bigger “scripts” or programs.
The console/terminal is bottom-left. The terminal is the 1980s window that does commands directly. Type print(‘Hello World’), press enter/return, and you’ve written some code. This console is what R would look like if you ran it without RStudio.
The top-right is the file manager panel. It shows files in your directory, plots, packages, and help files.
The bottom-right is the programming environment. It contains things you’ve loaded or coded. Right now it should be empty because you’ve done neither.

Of course all of these can be moved around to match how you like to set it up. And I need a different color-scheme than the ugly default.
Select the Tools menu, followed by Global Options in the dropdown Tools menu. Navigate to appearance and you should be able to alter the color scheme to something more your style. My preferred style is Merbivore Soft as shown in Figure A.2.






Figure A.2: goodRstudio
crumbs: A  R and RStudio Installation and Overview

title: Appendix B — Resources
text:
Base R is the collection of functions that come preloaded with R. A function is a code block that performs a task. Functions that come in R are in-built functions. However, the real power of R (and programming languages more generally) are the large number of user-built functions that are built to perform tasks that can be loaded into base R.
Sets of functions that have a common purpose are bundled together in R in “packages”. There are literally thousands of packages custom-built for R.
During this course, I will ask you to use some important packages for data acquisition, tidying, and visualization. There are two steps to using a package in R.
First, it needs to be installed. Here’s a non-functional example.

install.packages('<PACKAGE.NAME>')

And this example will install an actual package called janitor. This package features a nice import function that fixes column names on import called clean_names().

install.packages('janitor')

In a meta way, you now have used a function to install a package. ‘install.package()’ is an example of an in-built R function.
Once a package is installed, it needs to be loaded. While the installation has put the package on your computer, you need to tell R that it should load these functions for use.






R and other programming languages only do EXACTLY what they are told to do.



Loading a package is accomplished by calling base R library function. Success is indicated with a message like “Attaching package: ‘janitor’

library(janitor)


Attaching package: 'janitor'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    chisq.test, fisher.test


Libraries have to be loaded each time you restart your R session. The current libraries loaded can be founded in the file manager panel under the Packages tab.

B.0.1 Color Resources
Courtesy of Dalai Vo - Fall 2022 EA 078 student - a color palette cheatsheet

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
text:
C.1 Is there a typo?
Running any line of code or code block will fail if there is a typo.
Check for:

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
text:
typos or misspelling of variables, functions,
capitalization
missing punctuation (parenthesis, comma, pipe, +)

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
section: C.2 Is the package/library installed and loaded?
text:
C.2 Is the package/library installed and loaded?
If you are calling a function, the library needs to be installed and loaded (or called explicitly).

Check if the library is loaded in the Files, Plots, Packages... under the Packages tab. A package is loaded if there is a checkmark by it.
Check if the package is installed. Same procedure as above, but the package won’t even be listed if it isn’t installed.
Call the package explicitly using the syntax <PACKAGE.NAME>::<FUNCTION.NAME>. An example would be janitor::clean_names().

If you don’t know where the panels are - see this image and description. We’ll use it a few times.






Figure C.1: RStudio



Here, there are four separate windows or “Panels”.

The source panel is top-left and this is basically a text editor where you type R code or regular text. Code here gets colored and looks different if it is doing various R things. More on this later. Code doesn’t get immediately executed here; it is more like a holding place for writing/testing/debugging bigger “scripts” or programs.
The console/terminal is bottom-left. The terminal is the 1980s window that does commands directly. Type print(‘Hello World’), press enter/return, and you’ve written some code. This console is what R would look like if you ran it without RStudio.
The top-right is the file manager or Files, Plots, Packages... panel. It shows files in your directory, plots, packages, and help files.
The bottom-right is the programming environment or Environment panel. It contains things you’ve loaded or coded. Right now it should be empty because you’ve done neither.

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
section: C.3 Is the data loaded in the global environment?
text:
C.3 Is the data loaded in the global environment?
The data needs to be imported and loaded into the global environment before it can be manipulated.

Check the Environment panel to see if the dataset of interest is loaded and named as expected.

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
text:
C.4 Is the syntax correct?
The syntax for the code needs to be correct, and needs to follow the idiomatic rules of R.

Should it be a pipe |> or a +?
Does the function include all required arguments?
Did I pass in the data?

title: Appendix C — Debugging Steps
section: C.5 Does a simpler version work?
text:
C.5 Does a simpler version work?
When coding in class, we’ll often start simple, then add complexity for our visualization code. The reason for that is to make sure the very basic things are all there and working, and also because it makes debugging much simpler.
If a long piece of code breaks, try to see if a minimum reproducible example does work. This is standard for trouble-shooting to make sure none of the previous four things are the problem. Try to make it a single line of code with the most basic functionality and see if that works. Then add one line at a time to see where things break.
